assertions.justification: The response does not mention CRDT, OT, or any specific databases; it only asks clarifying questions about usage patterns.
The response explicitly asks about workload shape by presenting three distinct read/write concurrency scenarios (few writers many readers, distributed editing, true mass concurrent editing).
The response asks whether the 100k figure comes from business goals, product planning, or a rough estimate, implicitly probing constraints around scope and planning horizon.
The response explicitly questions whether the 100k concurrent users figure is a firm requirement or an estimate, and whether to design for it from day one or grow into it.
The response immediately dives into CRDT, OT, Yjs, specific databases (PostgreSQL, TiDB, Redis), and full architecture diagrams without any clarifying questions.
The response never asks about read vs write patterns or concurrency expectations; it assumes a full architecture spec and proceeds to design around it.
No questions are asked about budget, team preferences, or deployment environment; the response jumps directly to prescriptive technology choices.
The 100k concurrent users figure is treated as a hard spec to immediately design around, with concrete node counts and scaling math provided without any clarification.

summary: The skill-guided response scored 4/4 by asking clarifying questions before any architecture decisions, while the baseline scored 0/4 by immediately designing a full system around the unverified 100k figure.
